title: Roast leg of lamb
subtitle: Studded with garlic and rosemary, served with mint sauce
excerpt: A whole leg of lamb studded with garlic slivers and rosemary, roasted until pink and carved at the table. The classic British Easter joint.
sourceNotes:
Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136. Leg of mutton and lamb roasting entries.

Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845), Project Gutenberg. Roast leg of lamb method.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Stud the lamb up to 24 hours ahead, cover loosely and refrigerate. Bring to room temperature an hour before roasting.
recipe.temperatureNote: fan oven; reduce to 180 after the initial 20 minutes

glossaryTerms.definition: Leaving cooked meat in a warm place before carving, allowing the muscle fibres to relax and the juices to redistribute so the meat stays moist when cut.
Pushing slivers of garlic or sprigs of herbs into slits cut in the surface of meat, so the flavour works through the joint during roasting.

content.text: A whole leg of lamb is a generous joint: two kilograms feeds six people easily and asks very little of the cook. The meat is flavourful enough to need only garlic and rosemary pressed into small cuts across the surface.
Pink lamb is better than grey. A probe reading of 60 degrees C at the thickest part gives a warm pink centre; 65 degrees C gives pale pink throughout.
What you need
A paring knife is useful for making clean, deep slits in the meat for the garlic and rosemary.
Method
Stud and season
Make about 16 deep slits across the surface of the lamb with a paring knife. Press a sliver of garlic and a pinch of chopped rosemary into each slit. This is
studding
Rub the butter all over the joint and season generously with salt and pepper.
Roast
Heat the oven to 200 degrees C fan. Scatter the onion and carrot across the base of the roasting pan, pour over the stock, then set the rack on top. Place the lamb on the rack.
Roast for 20 minutes to colour the surface, then reduce to 180 degrees C. Continue roasting for about 60 more minutes, checking the temperature from 45 minutes onward.
Pull the lamb at 60 degrees C for pink throughout; at 65 degrees C for pale pink; at 72 degrees C for well done.
Rest and carve
Transfer to a warm plate and tent with foil.
Rest
for 20 minutes before carving.
Carve parallel to the bone, working in long slices from the rounded top side first, then turning the leg to carve the flatter underside.

items.symptom: The meat near the bone is still very pink when the outside is done
items.cause: The bone conducts heat slowly; the meat nearest it is always the last to cook.
items.fix: This is normal and correct for a pink roast. Check the temperature at the thickest part of the muscle, away from the bone.

items.symptom: The joint is dry and tight
items.fix: Use a probe thermometer. Lamb dries out quickly above 70 degrees C.

items.symptom: The garlic burnt on the surface
items.cause: The garlic slivers were pushed too shallow or sat proud of the surface.
items.fix: Push each sliver deep enough that it is covered by the meat. Any that sit at the surface will char in the oven.

items.symptom: The pan juices are thick and dark
items.cause: The stock evaporated and the vegetables scorched.
items.fix: Add another 100 ml of stock or water to the tin if the juices start to catch.

content.text: Variations

Butterflied leg (boned and opened flat) cooks in about 40 minutes at 200 degrees C and gives more brown surface to carve from. Rub the opened surface with a paste of garlic, rosemary, and a little lemon zest before cooking.
Where this dish lives

Roast leg of lamb arrived at the British Easter table partly because the new season lambs were ready in spring. Victorian cookery writer Mrs Beeton devotes a long section to it in the 1861 edition, recommending a flour dredge before roasting to crisp the surface. The garlic-and-rosemary stud is the twentieth-century French influence that pushed out the older English approach of plain basting.
